front.title: BGP best path next-hop selection enhancements

abstract.t: BGP [RFC4271] has originally been designed to carry IPv4 routing information over the Internet. IP routing being “hop-by-hop” in nature, [RFC4271] defines the NEXT_HOP attribute which purpose is to carry the address of the next router to send the IP packet to. In BGP, the next-hop may not be a directly connected router, hence, when evaluating paths, BGP needs to figure out if the next-hop is resolvable and, when needed, needs to figure out what is the internal cost to reach this next-hop.

The incremental use of tunneling technologies to carry traffic between routers (e.g.: GRE, MPLS, SR-MPLS, SRv6…) may violate the assumption that the address carried in the NEXT_HOP attribute is representative of the actual forwarding next-hop. These technologies decouple the BGP control-plane's view of the next-hop from the data-plane's actual forwarding endpoint. This document describes the problems that arise from this decoupling. These problems include sub-optimal path selection, incorrect resolvability tracking of the forwarding path leading to traffic blackholing, and others. This document proposes some modification of BGP path selection procedures to accommodate these use cases.

section.name: Requirements Language
section.t: The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "NOT RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 when, and only when, they appear in all capitals, as shown here.

section.name: Introduction
section.t: BGP is designed to exchange network reachability information between routing domains. A BGP update typically contains a prefix and a set of path attributes, including the well-known mandatory NEXT_HOP attribute. The receiving router uses this next-hop address to determine the egress point for traffic destined for the advertised prefix. The assumption is the next hop address represents the next router from a traffic forwarding point of view.

Section 9.1.2.1 defines the route resolvability condition: a BGP route is considered unresolvable if the BGP speaker’s routing table has no route matching the BGP route next-hop address. As per Section 9.1.2.2 e), when comparing paths received via internal BGP (IBGP), the Routing Table metric associated to the BGP route next-hop address is used to figure out the best path.

In , RTR3 receives prefix P/m from RTR1 and RTR2 (IBGP peers) with NEXT_HOP N1 and N2. RTR3 resolves N1 with a cost of 1 and with a cost of 2. Based on procedures, RTR3 will select the path with the lowest cost to the NEXT_HOP address, so RTR3 will select the path received from RTR1. In this example, the path used to resolve N1 and N2 reflects the actual forwarding path which makes the best path decision done by RTR3 accurate.

This document describes cases where the NEXT_HOP attribute used in the BGP update is not representative of the actual forwarding path. In these cases, the resolvability condition may fail in its goal and path selection may be done on inaccurate criteria leading to suboptimal routing, network congestion, traffic blackholing...
These use cases are not new and may have been partially addressed by IETF standards or drafts, some references are provided below:

ul.li: addresses the case of MPLS networks and proposes a modification of the route resolvability condition to be performed using forwarding database of a particular data plane protocol. It also proposes an optional data path verification.

Section 7 improves the route resolvability condition by verifying that there is a feasible tunnel. However, it doesn’t take into account that the cost associated with the tunnel may be different from the cost associated with the BGP next-hop.

defines SRv6 overlay services signaling using BGP. The procedures involve the advertisement of an SRv6 Service TLV within the BGP Prefix SID attribute to signal the SID to be used for forwarding. RFC9252 highlights that ingress PE must perform a resolvability check for the SRv6 SID in addition to the resolvability check done on the NEXT_HOP attribute. However, it doesn’t take into account that the cost associated with the tunnel may be different from the cost associated with the BGP next-hop.

section.t: This document defines generic modifications to the BGP decision process that can apply to all the use cases.

section.title: Segment Routing Traffic Engineering Policy
section.t: When using SR Policies , the BGP NEXT_HOP attribute may not accurately represent the actual forwarding path.

figure.title: Next hop attribute not used for forwarding

section.t: In the above figure, a BGP VPN service is deployed between the PEs. Let's assume PE3 receives prefix P/m from PE1 and PE2 with NEXT_HOP N1, N2 and color C1. On PE3, SR Policies (C1, N1) and (C1, N2) are configured to use the low latency path.

ul.li: Costs in parentheses represents cost of IGP.
Costs in brackets represents cost of latency-based metric

section.t: If PE3 performs the next-hop resolution solely based on the BGP NEXT_HOP attribute, it will not verify that an SR policy (C1, Nx) exists and is up before using it. It may also pick the wrong best path by considering the cost to the NEXT_HOP address instead of the cost of the SR policy. The best path considering SR-policy (C1, Nx) cost is via PE2, while the best path considering IGP metric is via PE1.

Considering that PE3 may take into account the cost of the SR policy and then picks up the path from PE2 as best. If the SR Policy (C1, N2) is down or not present, and considering that color C1 is using CO-flag equal to 00, path from PE2 is authorized to use IGP path to next-hop N2 (fallback to IGP path via P1 with cost=6) as there is no policy (C1, N2) available. This situation has multiple drawbacks:

ul.li: Cost of the two BGP paths are not comparable which makes the comparison inaccurate: PE1 path uses a cost based on latency while cost of path from PE2 uses IGP metric.
Using a path without an SR-policy may violate the intent of the service, especially if there is an alternate path (from PE1) that fulfills the intent.

section.t: It may be desirable for PE3 to prefer the path from PE1 that satisfies color 1 or even exclude any path that does not meet the color 1 requirement.

section.title: SRv6 services
section.t: When using SRv6 services as defined in , the BGP NEXT_HOP attribute may not be representative of the actual forwarding path.

Considering , an SRv6-based BGP VPN is deployed between the PEs. PE1 uses locator L1 for algorithm 0 and L1_FA for flexible algorithm 128 () optimized for low-latency. Simarly, PE2 uses locator L2 for algorithm 0 and L2_FA for flexible algorithm 128. Let's assume PE3 receives prefix P/m from PE1 and PE2 with NEXT_HOP N1, N2 and SRv6 SID S1 and S2. SID S1 and S2 are allocated respectively from L1_FA and L2_FA.

ul.li: Costs M in parentheses represents cost of algo 0 (as defined in Section 8.5).
Costs D in brackets represents cost of flexible algorithm 129 which is using low-latency metric.

section.t: The IPv6 routing table of PE3 contains the following entries:

Based on procedures, PE3 verifies that NEXT_HOP address of each path is resolvable. Based on procedures, PE3 verifies that SID1 and SID2 address are resolvable. PE3 will then select the path with the lowest cost to the NEXT_HOP address according to . Cost to N1 is lower than cost to N2, so PE3 will select the path received from PE1. However, from a latency perspective, path to PE2 is the best one.

The problem of path suboptimality may also happen with algorithm 0, if for instance SRv6 traffic for algorithm 0 needs to be offloaded from PE1, operator may increase the metric of the locator (while not changing the metric of the next-hop) on PE1. In the example above, if L1 is advertised by PE1 with an offset of 1M, then PE3 will have to cost to L1 of 1000002 but N1 will still be reachable with a cost of 2. The cost to reach the SID must also be taken into account in this scenario to ensure that the traffic offload works properly.

section.title: MPLS path
section.t: In , a BGP MPLS VPN is deployed over PEs. Let's assume PE3 receives prefix P/m from PE1 and PE2 with NEXT_HOP N1 and N2. The existence of a route in PE3's IPv4 routing table to reach N1 or N2 (defined in ) is not sufficient to ensure that traffic can be carried from PE3 to PE1 or PE2. PE3 needs to ensure that there is a tunnel available to reach PE1 or PE2 that can carry MPLS traffic (e.g.: an MPLS Label Switched Path (LSP)). By checking only for the existence of a route in its routing table, PE3 could create a traffic blackhole if there is no tunnel to carry the MPLS VPN traffic.

section.title: Modification of the BGP route selection process
Forwarding address
section.t: This document defines the forwarding address as the IP address of the next hop that will be used to forward packets. The forwarding address may come from the NEXT_HOP or a different address which has been signaled in a different attribute along with the path.
The following data already defined in BGP standards SHOULD be considered as forwarding addresses:

ul.li: The Tunnel Egress Endpoint Sub-TLV contained in Tunnel Encapsulation attribute defined in
SRv6 SID Information Sub-TLV contained within L3 or L2 SRv6 SID defined in

section.t: Unless a BGP update contains another type of forwarding address, the BGP NEXT_HOP attribute is considered as the forwarding address.

A forwarding address MAY be accompanied by additional data (we call it forwarding context) that characterizes more the forwarding path to be used. As an example, defines encapsulation sub-TLVs: the information contained in these TLVs can be used as additional context to the forwarding address. When is used, it is easy to assume that the forwarding context is SRv6 forwarding.

Each new BGP extension SHOULD specify if an address carried by the extension must be considered as a forwarding address. The procedures defined in the next section SHOULD apply for any new forwarding address defined without having to redefine them.

section.title: Lookup table/Resolution filters
section.t: When the forwarding address and its context are identified for a BGP route, the implementation knows the characteristics of the forwarding path to be used. Each implementation has its own way of storing the various types of forwarding paths: they may be separated into different tables or share the same table.

Each implementation needs to figure out the proper internal table to use based on the forwarding address/context and, if needed, the filters to apply on the route lookup results. An example of filter could be that the route lookup result should have an MPLS outgoing path, or should be a tunnel (of any type) or resolution cannot be done on a prefix which mask is less than /32.

The BGP update may not provide enough context to determine the constraints of the forwarding path. Reusing the example defined in , if we consider an IPv4 unicast BGP service carried over a BGP free-core, the BGP NEXT_HOP must be reachable through a tunnel to allow the end-to-end packet delivery. Such a case cannot be derived from the BGP update context and BGP must be configured to resolve the NEXT_HOP only through tunnels (of any or specific types). An implementation MAY provide a set of configuration options to resolution table and/or resolution filters.

section.title: Route resolvability condition
section.t: This document updates Section 9.1.2.1 as follows:

ul.li: The route resolvability check for the BGP NEXT_HOP attribute MUST continue to be done.
In addition, the route resolvability criteria SHOULD be performed based on the forwarding address.
When resolving the forwarding address, look up SHOULD be performed in the table identified from the forwarding address and context.

section.title: Internal cost determination
section.t: For a prefix P/m, different BGP paths may use different forwarding addresses and contexts of various types.

figure.artwork: P/m Path1: NH=R1 Color: 200 Path2: NH=R2 SRv6-SID: R2_SID Path3: NH=R3 Tunnel-encap(L2TPv3, endpoint: R3)

section.t: Costs retrieved from different types of forwarding addresses or contexts may not be comparable because they are based on different sets of rules. For instance, path1 may leverage an SR-TE policy (color 200, endpoint R1) optimizing for latency, so the cost of path1 will reflect the latency to R1. Path2 may use the IGP cost to R2. Path3 may have no cost. These values are not directly comparable.

In order to compare the paths, this document introduces the concept of forwarding address preference. The preference value may come from the table lookup, or BGP local configuration may override it (for example, SR policies with color 200 may have the highest preference, while L2TP tunnels may have the lowest preference).
This document updates Section 9.1.2.2 e) as follows:

ul.li: Remove from consideration any routes with less forwarding address preference. The forwarding preference of a path is determined by either retrieval from the appropriate table identified in or by local BGP configuration.

For remaining paths, the interior cost of a route is determined by retrieving the metric to the forwarding address for the route from the appropriate table identified in . If the forwarding address for a route is reachable, but no cost can be determined, the cost SHOULD be set by default to the maximum allowed cost.

section.t: Using forwarding preference and forwarding address-based cost SHOULD be enabled through a configuration knob.

section.title: Example_SLI
section.t: The example below illustrates the logic of forwarding address preference and cost comparison.

A prefix P/m is reachable by PE4 from PE1, PE2, PE3 (with respectively NEXT_HOP N1, N2 and N3). Considering that the network is a BGP free core, traffic must be tunneled between edge devices. Traffic destined to P/m is of high bandwidth and requires traffic-engineering to spread the traffic across the available links of the core. RSVP-TE is used to provide traffic-engineering MPLS tunnels. SR-MPLS is also used to provide best-effort reachability. RSVP-TE is given a better preference (higher value in this example) than SR-MPLS. RSVP-TE tunnel to PE3 cannot be established.

table.name: MPLS ingress tunnel table of PE4

section.t: As mentioned in , the case of BGP free-core requires BGP on PE4 to be configured to allow the resolution the NEXT_HOP address through tunnels (of any type). Considering that PE4 maintains a separate table for MPLS ingress tunnels, PE4 will look up for N1, N2, N3 addresses only in this table. PE4 will first check the resolvability of N1, N2 and N3. All are resolvable in the MPLS ingress tunnel table. PE4 will end-up with the following information from the MPLS ingress tunnel table to compare the path:

figure.artwork:
P/m Path1: from PE1, NH=N1 cost 1000, preference 250
Path2: from PE2, NH=N2 cost 100, preference 250
Path3: from PE3, NH=NH3 cost 20, preference 110

section.t: PE4 will check the forwarding address preference of the paths. PE4 will not consider the paths received from PE3 because the forwarding address preference is lower than others. Finally, PE4 will compare the internal costs between paths from PE1 and PE2 as they have the same preference and path from PE2 will be elected as best because it has the lowest cost.

section.title: Example
section.t: The example below illustrates the logic of forwarding address preference and cost comparison.

In the above figure, IWG device is acting as an Inter Working Gateway (IWG) between an SRv6 and an MPLS-based BGP VPN. Let's assume IWG receives prefix P/m in the SRv6 domain from PE1, PE2 (with respectively NEXT_HOP N1, SRv6 SID SID1 and NEXT_HOP N2, SID2), and in the MPLS domain from PE3 and PE4 (with respectively NEXT_HOP N3 and N4) BGP is configured on IWG to use forwarding address preference and forwarding address-based cost. SRv6 transport is given the best preference.

ul.li: SRv6 domain is using flexible algorithm 129 which is using low-latency metric and MPLS domain is using algo 0.
Costs M in parentheses represents cost of algo 0 (as defined in Section 8.5).
Costs D in brackets represents cost of flexible algorithm 129 which is using low-latency metric.
PE1, PE2 and IWG are in the SRv6 domain, PE3, PE4 and IWG are in the MPLS domain.

section.t: IWG will first check the resolvability of N1, N2, N3 and N4. All are resolvable. Then, IWG will check the forwarding address preference of the paths. Let's assume that the forwarding address preference of SRv6 paths is higher than the one of MPLS paths. N3 and N4 will not be considered because the forwarding address preference of the MPLS path is lower than the SRv6 path. Then, IWG will check the resolvability of S1 and S2. If both paths are resolvable, IWG will compare the internal costs. and then use the path received from PE2 because the cost to reach S2 (1) is lower than the cost to reach S1 (5).

If S1 and S2 are not resolvable and no other SRv6 paths are available, IWG will consider the second forwarding address preference, and evaluate those paths. In this case, IWG will compare the cost to reach N3 (2) and N4 (6) and then use the path received from PE3 because the cost to reach N3 (2) is lower than the cost to reach N4 (6).

section.name: IANA Considerations
section.t: This document does not require any IANA actions.

section.name: Security Considerations
section.t: This document modifies BGP route selection process by using data other than the next-hop address to perform the resolvability check as well as to compute the internal cost. This does not add any security consideration compared to using the existing NEXT_HOP attribute defined in .
